title: VIBRADoor — Wireless Vibrating Doorbell for Deaf Students
desc: ESP32 + MQTT wireless doorbell system for deaf students — a commissioned build. A bell button at the classroom door sends a vibration alert to a wearable wristband worn by the student. One pair is deployed and in daily use at SLB Damayanti Sleman, Yogyakarta. Total BOM: Rp 366k per pair.

photos.caption: A student at SLB Damayanti using VIBRADoor in class — wearable on the wrist, transmitter at the desk. In daily use since 2025.

detail.challenge: A commissioned project for a special-needs school (SLB): deaf students cannot hear the classroom doorbell, causing missed visitors and miscommunication between staff and students. Conventional alternatives such as blinking lights are easy to miss when a student is focused on work or facing away from the door.
approach: As part of a 2-person team, my main contributions were the ESP32 firmware (Arduino framework) and the hardware assembly of both devices.

Two ESP32 DevKit nodes communicate through a self-hosted Mosquitto MQTT broker: a transmitter (R13-507 push button at the classroom door, 5V/1A adapter with an LM2598 step-down and an indicator LED) and a wearable wristband (ESP32 + 2000mAh LiPo + TP4056 charging module + 10mm DC vibration motor in a plastic enclosure).

Chose MQTT (pub/sub) over direct pairing such as ESP-NOW — one button can publish to many wristbands if the school adds units later, and each node's connection status can be monitored through the broker.
Double-sided perfboard PCB with 2.54mm JST connectors and pin headers for modularity: wearable components can be swapped or serviced without re-soldering — important for field maintenance.
Wristband power management: 2000mAh LiPo charged through a TP4056 via a 5.5×2.1mm DC jack — lasts a full school day per charge, light and safe for students to wear.
Total BOM of Rp 366k per pair (transmitter + wearable) — affordable and sustainable to replicate on follow-up requests.
outcome: One pair (transmitter + wearable) deployed and still in daily use at SLB Damayanti Sleman, Yogyakarta since 2025 — reliability proven in a real environment, not just a lab prototype.
Reduced communication friction between staff and deaf students: the student feels the vibration on their wrist without having to watch the door.
The MQTT architecture scales: adding pairs for other doors or students only requires provisioning to the same topic, with no base-firmware changes.
Commissioned-trust proof: entrusted to build a custom IoT device for a specific real-world need — beyond academic projects.

title: PAYCAY — Smart EV Charging Station (IoT + Web)
desc: End-to-end EV charging station (SPKLU) management system: ESP32 firmware (PZEM-004T + RC522 RFID + AC relay), Flask + Firebase Realtime DB backend, real-time web dashboard over Socket.IO, deployed on Microsoft Azure App Service.

detail.challenge: Build unattended EV-charging infrastructure: the station's ESP32 must read the energy meter and RFID, control an AC relay, and stay in sync with the admin/user web dashboard — all in real time, without polling, and accessible from anywhere.
approach: ESP32 firmware (Arduino) combines a PZEM-004T (V/I/P/E), a 13.56 MHz RC522 RFID reader for tap-to-start, a 16x2 LCD for local status, and a 10A relay for AC power control.

Flask backend with a service layer built on an Abstract Base Class (DatabaseInterface) — easy to swap database implementations; @login_required and @admin_required decorators for multi-role authorization.
Flask-SocketIO (gevent worker) streams sensor updates to the browser live; Firebase Realtime DB stores station state readable by both the firmware and the web.
Deployed on Azure App Service (Indonesia Central) with gunicorn + GeventWebSocketWorker and GitHub Actions CI/CD; ESP32 captive-portal AP for initial WiFi setup.
outcome: 1st place, Produk IoT category, at ARCADIA 2026 (Faculty of Computer Science, Universitas Amikom Yogyakarta).
Live at paycay-app.azurewebsites.net with an admin dashboard (station & user management, statistics) and a user dashboard (live charging, history, top-up).
Automatic balance deduction based on kWh from the PZEM-004T; remote start/stop from the web without being at the station.
Applied advanced Python concepts: OOP, ABCs, decorators, type hints, design patterns (Singleton, Factory, Strategy), real-time WebSocket.
Complete REST API for the session lifecycle: start → update (live sensor) → end, plus RFID-UID authentication.

title: PAYCAY Mobile — Companion App
desc: Flutter companion app for the PAYCAY SPKLU system. Real-time ESP32 (PZEM-004T) sensor monitoring via Firebase RTDB, a digital wallet, and an admin panel — separate User and Admin sides.

detail.role: Solo Developer · Mobile (companion to IoT + Web)
detail.challenge: Bring the full PAYCAY feature set to mobile: real-time station monitoring, charging-session management, wallet & top-up, and admin approvals — without relying on direct Bluetooth/WiFi links between the hardware and the phone.
approach: Firebase Realtime Database acts as the message broker between the ESP32 (sensor writer) and the mobile app (reader) — mirroring the web dashboard in PRJ_002.
Clean architecture with Riverpod 2.x: Presentation → Services → Firebase SDKs; StreamProvider for RTDB/Firestore, StateNotifierProvider for the active session persisted via SharedPreferences.
In-app sensor simulator so the app can be tested and demoed before the physical station exists; it steps aside automatically when the ESP32 comes online, with no UI changes.
Atomic Firestore transactions for the balance (top-up & charging cost) keep it consistent under concurrent writes.
outcome: Complete dual-role app (User & Admin) with UI polish: shimmer loading, empty states, pull-to-refresh.
Session recovery: the app can be killed mid-charge and reopened — the active-session banner returns with a consistent elapsed time.
Auto-stop on empty balance works end-to-end (2-second sensor poll → cost ≥ balance → relay stop command via RTDB).
Ready to pair with the ESP32 firmware in PRJ_002 — identical RTDB/Firestore schemas.

title: Presensi SMPN 2 Paliyan — IoT School Attendance (RFID + ESP32)
desc: IoT school attendance system for SMPN 2 Paliyan — a campus/community project with the school as partner. Students tap an RFID card on an ESP32 gate device (or enter their NIPD on the keypad as a fallback), data flows to a Flask server, and attendance is monitored in real time through an admin dashboard and a parent portal. The device is installed and in daily use.

photos.caption: RFID + keypad attendance device installed at the SMPN 2 Paliyan gate — used for daily attendance.

detail.challenge: SMPN 2 Paliyan needed reliable, transparent attendance: manual recaps were slow, parents had no visibility into their child's attendance, and the school's network is not always stable — the device must keep recording even when the LAN or server is down.
approach: My contributions span three sides: the ESP32 firmware, the gate-device hardware assembly (RC522 RFID, 3×4 keypad, 16×2 LCD, DS3231 RTC, W5500 Ethernet module, SD card), and the backend server (Flask + Firebase Firestore).
Attendance flow: tap an RFID card, or type the NIPD on the keypad when a card is forgotten or lost; the attendance status shows immediately on the LCD.

Offline resilience (store-and-forward): when the LAN/server is down, taps are stored on the SD card and re-sent automatically once back online; time syncs via NTP with the DS3231 RTC as an offline fallback.
Power backup (UPS): the device keeps running and recording attendance through school power outages — complementing store-and-forward so no tap is lost.
Unattended 24/7 reliability: a watchdog (self-reboot if the program hangs) plus a scheduled daily reboot, since the device runs continuously at the gate.

The Flask backend I built is the single gateway to data: neither the device nor the browser touches Firestore directly — access goes through a service account (Security Rules deny client access), with a REST API for attendance taps, student data, and reports.
outcome: Installed and used for daily attendance at SMPN 2 Paliyan — running in a real environment, not a lab prototype.
RFID/keypad taps stay reliably recorded through network drops and power outages, thanks to SD-card store-and-forward and the UPS backup.
The backend powers the admin dashboard (recaps, roll-call, charts, Excel import, PDF/Excel/CSV export, grade promotion) and the public parent portal (attendance lookup by NISN).
